name: Scope Graphs
summary: Name resolution as a graph-query problem: binding structure is represented as graph edges (declarations, references, imports, parents) and resolving a name is a traversal. Néron-Tolmach-Visser-Wachsmuth introduced the formalism; Spoofax productionized it for editor tooling and language workbenches.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Scope-graph approaches represent binding structure as graph edges and resolve names through graph queries. The Néron–Tolmach–Visser–Wachsmuth scope-graph framework formalized this view; subsequent work and Spoofax tooling have built on it. The approach is attractive for IDEs because it lets editor features ask for exactly the semantic fact needed at a cursor location instead of forcing whole-program analysis.

The distinguishing feature versus a traditional symbol table is that the binding structure is *first-class data*: edges are explicit, queries are reified, and the resolution algorithm is a graph traversal that can be memoized at any granularity.

sections.title: Graph elements and resolution as query
IDE and incrementality fit
sections.content:
Query-based compilers and language servers often memoize name-resolution and type-checking facts so that edits invalidate only affected facts. Scope graphs fit this discipline naturally: a path query over a graph is exactly the kind of thing a demand-driven framework like Salsa can memoize at the node level.

For a language designer, the practical lesson is that **if name resolution is reified as a graph + queries from the start, an IDE-quality language server costs much less to build than if name resolution lives only as imperative code in a batch-compiler pass**. Spoofax exploits this for language workbenches; rust-analyzer uses a related demand-driven approach without the explicit graph reification.
